coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ion using ultra-small fluorescent carbon dots derived from pineapple (Ananas comosus): Development of miniaturized analytical method
coredata.dc:creator: Gupta, Disha Anilbhai
Desai, Mittal L.
Malek, Naved I.
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Herein, water soluble nanosize carbon dots (CDs) were derived from pineapple (Ananas comosus) via acid-oxidation method using sulphuric and phosphoric acids as oxidizing agents. The synthesized CDs showed three fluorescence colors i.e., blue (B-CDs), green (G-CDs) and, yellow (Y-CDs) under UV-lamp. Spectroscopic techniques (fluorescence, UV–visible and Fourier-transform infrared) were used for the investigation of surface chemistry of three CDs and their analytical applications. The three different fluorescent CDs probes were subjected to metal ions selectivity study where the fluorescence of B-CDs was significantly quenched by one of the metal ion i.e., Fe3+, which indicates that the formation of Fe3+ ion - B-CDs complex. The rapid fluorescence quenching occurs when Fe3+ ion is added. The fluorescence quenching exhibited a linear response for Fe3+ ion (0.05–500 μM) and a detection limit of 0.03 μM. The B-CDs-based fluorescence “turn-off” method may open up a facile and sensitive analytical approach for fluorescence assay of Fe3+ ion in biofluids and environmental matrices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent CDs
Fe3+ ion
Pineapple
Acid oxidation
HR-TEM
Spectroscopic techniques

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence sensing ability of three CDs
Fe3+ ion analysis in urine, drug and plasma

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Spectral (UV–visible absorption and fluorescence) characteristics of fluorescent (a) B-, (b) G- and (c) Y- CDs.
HR-TEM images and corresponding histogram of (a) B-, (b) G- and (c) Y- CDs at 5 nm scale bar obtained from Ananas comosus fruit.

Changes in the emission spectra of B-CDs with addition of metal ions (Co2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr3+ and Fe3+, 500 μM). Inset: Blue fluorescent color CDs changes with the above metal ions under UV-light.
Linear quenching of emission peak of B-CDs with Fe3+ (1–500 μM). Inset: Linear response between the I0–I/I0 values of B-CDs and Fe3+ ion concentration (0.05–100 μM).
Synthesis of nanosized three color CDs from Ananas comosus fruit via acid oxidation.
Comparison of present method with the reported methods for assaying of Fe3+ ion with regard to linear range and LOD.
Comparison of present method with different types of analytical strategies for the detection of Fe3+ ion.

row.entry: Carbon dots from Ananas Comosus fruit

head.ce:title: Fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ion using ultra-small fluorescent carbon dots derived from pineapple (Ananas comosus): Development of miniaturized analytical method

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat, 395 007, Gujarat, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: S. V. National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Applied Chemistry Department, S. V. National Institute of Technology, Surat, 395 007, Gujarat, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, water soluble nanosize carbon dots (CDs) were derived from pineapple (Ananas comosus) via acid-oxidation method using sulphuric and phosphoric acids as oxidizing agents. The synthesized CDs showed three fluorescence colors i.e., blue (B-CDs), green (G-CDs) and, yellow (Y-CDs) under UV-lamp. Spectroscopic techniques (fluorescence, UV–visible and Fourier-transform infrared) were used for the investigation of surface chemistry of three CDs and their analytical applications. The three different fluorescent CDs probes were subjected to metal ions selectivity study where the fluorescence of B-CDs was significantly quenched by one of the metal ion i.e., Fe3+, which indicates that the formation of Fe3+ ion - B-CDs complex. The rapid fluorescence quenching occurs when Fe3+ ion is added. The fluorescence quenching exhibited a linear response for Fe3+ ion (0.05–500 μM) and a detection limit of 0.03 μM. The B-CDs-based fluorescence “turn-off” method may open up a facile and sensitive analytical approach for fluorescence assay of Fe3+ ion in biofluids and environmental matrices.

ce:section.ce:para: Among the ultra-small fluorescent nanomaterials, CDs have evolved as a new class of non-toxic fluorescent nanomaterials with outstanding applications in optics, bioimaging, sensing and environment remediation [1]. The origin of fluorescent nature of CDs is due to the surface functional groups, quasi-spherical shape, small size (<10 nm), surface defects and graphitic nature, exploring them as alternative materials to semiconductor nanomaterials [2]. Scrivens’s group was first noticed that the fluorescence emission properties of carbon nanomaterials through the preparation of single walled carbon nanotube via arc-discharge method [2]. Later on, Sun and co-workers first called as “carbon dots” due to their small size and unique optical properties [3]. The fluorescence nature of CDs is mainly dependent on the various parameters including synthetic protocol, carbon sources, dopant, temperature and pH [4,5]. Although a wide variety of synthetic approaches has been explored for the fabrication of nanosize fluorescent carbon dots, the hydrothermal and acid oxidation routes routes have proven to be green chemistry methods due to their simplicity, carbonization using natural resources and inexpensiveness [6,7]. To support this, nanosize non-toxic CDs have been obtained by hydrothermal method using various inexpensive plant materials as carbon sources including milk [8], apple juice [9], potato [10], Punica granatum [11], dried shrimp [12], sugar cane juice [13], water melon peel [14], corn flour [15], jamun [16], orange juice [17], chicken egg [18], Manilkara zapota [19] and tomato [20] fruits, respectively. These synthetic strategies have proven to be the efficient green chemistry approaches for the fabrication of nanosize CDs and their applications in diverse field of science.

It is well known that iron is one of the important biological elements that involves various biochemical reactions in the living system. It served as a co-factor for various enzymatic reactions and acted as vehicle for electrons transport in plants and animals [8]. However, it causes severe health disorders when it is either in lower or in higher concentrations. Over the past few years, the integration of fluorescent CDs with fluorescence spectroscopy has shown as a promising analytical platform for assaying of wide variety chemical species with impressive detection limits [21]. Further, Fe3+ ion was efficiently assayed by using ultras-small CDs derived from various natural resources i.e., Syzygium cumini [22], potato [23], honey [24], Jinhua bergamot [25], garlic [26], Prunus avium fruit [27], rose-heart radish [28] and Lycii Fructus [29], respectively. To improve the sensing performance of CDs, various chemical compounds (citric acid and ethylenediamine) [30] and Si [31] were used as reagents for synthesis of CDs and used as a fluorescent nanosensor for Fe3+ ion sensing. Furthermore, the fluorescent CDs were derived from citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline [32] and ethanol [33] and used as probes for detection of ferric ion. Dicationic imidazolium salts were synthesized for the development of fluorescence analytical method for assaying of Fe3+ ion in the presence of other ions [34]. A new fluorescent polymer material was developed by modifying with propane-1,3-dione and 2,5-diethynylbenzene derivatives for assaying of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions [35]. Zhou’s group prepared a series of cyclophane derivatives and studied their fluorescence detection ability towards Fe3+ and H2PO4 − [36]. Debajani and co-workers studied the fluorescence properties of two amino-alkyl receptor with two different rhodamine derivatives for selective absorption and fluorescence responses towards Fe3+ ion [37]. Several research groups have been synthesized photoluminescence CDs with high quantum yield for assaying of Fe3+ ion [38–41]. Apart from this, ultra-small CDs have also been applied as promising agents for the development of fluorescence analytical method for assaying of various metal ions (Cu2+, Al3+, Hg2+, and Ag+ ions) [7,16,42,43].

Herein, first time, pineapple (Ananas comosus, family: Bromeliaceae) fruit was used as a novel and potential carbon precursor for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs via acid oxidation (Scheme 1 ). It is a tropical fruit that contains various phytochemicals i.e., carbohydrate, bromelain, polyphenols and vitamins. The prepared CDs exhibited three fluorescent colors i.e., blue, green and yellow, and represented as B-, G- and Y- CDs. The prepared CDs exhibited three different emission peaks (438, 516 and 543 nm) upon their excitation at 325, 417 and 425 nm. Interestingly, the selectivity study with different metal ions showed that only Fe3+ metal ion was remarkably quenched the emission intensity at 438 nm, revealing the B-CDs act as fluorescent sensor for assay of Fe3+ ion. Owing to their specific interaction ability, the B-CDs are applied as an ideal fluorescent probe for the sensitive and selective fluorescence assay of Fe3+ ion, exhibiting a wider linear range (0.05–500 μM). The developed platform offers a facile analytical approach for the assay of Fe3+ ion in water and biofluids, offering a facile, selective and sensitive miniaturized platform for Fe3+ ion assay with lower detection limit.

Pineapple fruit was bought from a local market in Surat, India. Sulphuric acid (H2SO4, 95%), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), metal salts, and NaOH were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. All standard solutions were prepared by using Milli-Q water. The absorption spectra of CDs were carried out on Maya Pro 2000 spectrometer, Ocean Optics, USA. Fluorescence spectrometric studies were measured on Cary Eclipse Fluorescence Spectrometer, Agilent Technologies, USA. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were carried on Alpha II FT-IR spectrometer (Bruker Optics GmbH, Germany). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) (JEM- 2100, JEOL, Japan) was used to measure the size and morphology of nanosize CDs derived from pineapple.

Ultra-small three color (blue, green and yellow) non-toxic CDs were derived from pineapple via one-step acid oxidation method [19,44]. Typically, out layer of pineapple was removed and cut into small pieces. The pineapple pieces were kept at −23 °C for frozen. The B-CDs were prepared by the following procedure. 0.5 g of frozen pineapple was taken into a reaction flask that contain 5.0 mL of deionized H2O and the sample was sonicated for 5 min. To this, 10 mL of 34 N sulphuric acid was carefully added and the reaction mixture was heated at 100 °C for 60 min. Then, the mixture was kept aside for few minutes to reach ambient temperature. It is noticed that the reaction mixture was turned into black color. Then, NaOH (1.0 M) was used to neutralize 1.0 mL of crude solution. The blue fluorescent CDs were obtained by dialyzing it against deionized water using dialysis membrane-70 for 24 h. Similarly, G-CDs and Y-CDs were prepared by dissolving 0.5 g of frozen pineapple separately into 10 mL of 40 N H3PO4 and 5 mL of water for G-CDs, whereas 10 mL of 40 N H3PO4 was used for Y-CDs. After adding these, the samples were heated at 80 °C for 20–30 and 20 min for G- and Y- nanosize CDs and then the formed crude was treated with NaOH (1.0 M). The nanosize three colors CDs were isolated by dialyzing the above solution against deionized H2O using dialysis membrane-70 for 24 h and obtained nanosize three color CDs were stored in the dark at 4 °C for further use.

The fluorescence assay of Fe3+ ion was performed by using B-CDs as a probe. Typically, 1.0 mL of B-CDs were successively taken into a 5.0 mL glass vials and then 700 μL of Fe3+ ion with different concentrations (0.05–500 μM) were added separately and then vortexed for 60 s. The sample vials were incubated for 15 min and their spectra were recorded for the construction of calibration graph. To examine the selectivity of B-CDs, Co2+, Al3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, and Mg2+ (500 μM, 700 μL), totally 11 metal ions were introduced into B-CDs and studied their spectral characteristics.

To extend the practical application of the method, the developed probed was applied for assaying of Fe3+ ion in urine, serum and tablets. To this, urine, and serum were treated with Fe3+ ion (10, 50 and 100 μM) and the Fe3+ ion concentration was determined by the aforesaid procedure. Moreover, the application of the present method was also applied to detect Fe3+ ion in iron-supplement tablets. Iron supplement tablets were procured from Local Medical Shop in Surat, Gujarat. The iron tablets were powdered and then dissolved in deionized water. The solution was filtered and the amount of Fe3+ ion was estimated by the earlier described procedure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence sensing ability of three CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the recognition ability of three CDs, various target metal species (Co2+, Cu2+, Al3+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Hg2+, Mg2+, Zn2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Fe2+ and Fe3+, 0.7 mL; 500 μM) were introduced into 1.0 mL of B-CDs and their emission spectra were measured at 438 nm upon the excitation wavelength of 325 nm (Fig. 3 ). Similarly, the recognition ability of G- and Y- CDs was also investigated and the measured spectra were shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S4. These results revealed that only Fe3+ ion has significantly quenched the emission spectra of B-CDs whereas other metal species did not decline the emission intensity, demonstrating the specific recognition ability of B-CDs towards Fe3+ ion. It was seen that the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs is higher than that of G- and Y- CDs (Table S1), confirming the Fe3+ ion has shown higher quenching rates only for B-CDs. Thus, analytical method was established for sensing of Fe3+ ion using B-CDs as a probe via fluorescence quenching mechanism.

SEC; Size exclusion chromatography, HPLC; High performance liquid chromatography, ICPMS; Inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy, FAAS; flame atomic absorption spectrometry, ICP-AES; Inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry.

The fluorescence quenching of nano size B-CDs is related to the groups of −COOH, −OH, −NH2, which possibly due to the strong interaction with Fe3+ ion. The organic groups on B-CDs were effectively coordinated with Fe3+ ion to B-CDs-Fe3+ ion nanoaggregates, which results to drastic change in the morphology and size of B-CDs (Supporting Information of Figs. S3d and S6). These results illustrated that Fe3+ ion was effectively make the chelation with surface organic groups of B-CDs, resulting to induce the aggregation of B-CDs via the formation of B-CDs-Fe3+ ion complexes, which favours to quench the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs.

To examine the selectivity of nanosize B-CDs, various kinds of chemical species including metal ions (Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Ni2+ and Al3+, 500 μM), anions (SO4 2−, Cl−, Br−, PO4 3− and Cr2O7 2−, 500 μM) and pesticides (glyophosate, acetamiprid, chlorpropham, tebuconazole and imidacloprid, 500 μM) were examined with and without Fe3+ ion (500 μM). It was observed that the addition 0.7 mL of mixture of different chemical species to 1.0 mL of B-CDs did not quench significantly the emission intensity of nanosize B-CDs, but it quenches only with Fe3+ ion (500 μM) (Supporting Information of Fig. S7), confirming the selectivity of nanosize B-CDs for sensing of Fe3+ ion in the competitive environment.

The fluorescence quenching of nano size B-CDs is related to the groups of −COOH, −OH, −NH2, which possibly due to the strong interaction with Fe3+ ion. The organic groups on B-CDs were effectively coordinated or chelated with Fe3+ ion to B-CDs-Fe3+ ion nanoaggregates, which results to drastic change in the morphology and size of B-CDs (Supporting Information of Figures of S3d and S6). These results demonstrated that the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs by Fe3+ ion is due to the aggregation of B-CDs induced by Fe3+ ion. In order to explain the detection mechanism by inner filter effect (IFE), the spectra (absorption, excitation and emission) of Fe3+ ion, B-CDs and B-CDs-Fe3+ ion were shown in Supporting Information of Fig. S8. Since the IFE involves the overlapping of absorption peak of Fe3+ ion (absorber) and the excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs (fluorophore). The excitation and emission spectra of B-CDs show bands at 325 and 438 nm whereas the absorption band of Fe3+ ion shows at 315 nm. These spectral characteristics demonstrated that the absorption spectrum of Fe3+ ion is maximum overlapped with excitation spectrum of B-CDs, confirming the fluorescence quenching of B-CDs induced by absorbance enhancement of Fe3+ ion, which favours the IFE pathway during the sensing Fe3+ ion using B-CDs as a sensor. Thus, based on HR-TEM and spectral characteristics, the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ion using B-CDs is based on the aggregation of B-CDs induced by Fe3+ ion and IFE.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe3+ ion analysis in urine, drug and plasma
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the practical application of the method for the analysis of Fe3+ ion in real samples, Fe3+ ion analysis was carried out in urine, drug and plasma samples. Briefly, 0.5 mL of Fe3+ ion (10, 50 and 100 μM) was treated with urine, drug and plasma. The Fe3+ ion amount was calculated by the described procedure earlier. Table S2 of Supporting Information reveals that the method exhibited good recoveries (98.7–102.6%) with relative standard deviation values 0.27–2.18%, revealing the potentiality of nanosize-B-CDs integrated fluorescence approach for recognition of Fe3+ ion in plasma, urine and drug samples at low volume samples.

In summary, a simple, and efficient green chemical route was introduced for the generation of different colored nanosize fluorescent CDs using pineapple (Ananas comosus) as a carbon source via acid oxidation. Accordingly, B-, G- and Y- fluorescent CDs showed emission peaks at 438, 516 and 543 nm by exciting them at 325, 417 and 425 nm, respectively. The B-CDs showed remarkable affinity towards Fe3+ ion, yielding a drastic decrease in the emission intensity at 438 nm via Fe3+ ion-nanosize-B-CDs nano aggregations. The B-CDs integrated fluorescence approach is a rapid, simple and environment friendly for the identification of Fe3+ ion even at as low a concentration as 0.05 μM without any complicated procedure. Finally, this study may provide wider interest in developing miniaturized analytical platforms for Fe3+ ion sensing and bioimaging using CDs as non-toxic fluorescent probes.

Gupta Disha Anilbhai: She has carried out entire experimental work and written the experimental sections of manuscript. Mittal L. Desai: She has characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. Naved I. Malek: He is involved in the discussion of DLS data. Suresh Kumar Kailasa: Completely supervised the work.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Department of Science and Technology, Government of India (EMR/2016/002621/IPC).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molstruc.2020.128343.
